runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 2 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates
CBO microsimulation documentation - labor supply elasticities by income group
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation parameterization
runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in the second earnings decile, the substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit microsimulation reflects how responsive these relatively low-earning primary earners are to changes in the effective marginal wage. Primary earners generally have lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. However, at decile 2 (near the bottom of the earnings distribution), workers may be somewhat more responsive than median earners due to proximity to extensive-margin decisions and interactions with phase-ins/phase-outs of transfer programs (e.g., EITC). Typical microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, TAXSIM-adjacent models) use substitution elasticities for primary earners in the range of 0.1–0.3, with lower-income deciles sometimes assigned modestly higher values than middle-income deciles. Gruber and Saez (2002) find compensated elasticities of taxable income around 0.12–0.40 depending on specification and income group. Balancing these considerations, I center my estimate at 0.20 with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45.

(2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation parameterization"], "reasoning_summary": "For primary earners in the second earnings decile, the substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit microsimulation reflects how responsive these relatively low-earning primary earners are to changes in the effective marginal wage. Primary earners generally have lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. However, at decile 2 (near the bottom of the earnings distribution), workers may be somewhat more responsive than median earners due to proximity to extensive-margin decisions and interactions with phase-ins/phase-outs of transfer programs (e.g., EITC). Typical microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, TAXSIM-adjacent models) use substitution elasticities for primary earners in the range of 0.1\u20130.3, with lower-income deciles sometimes assigned modestly higher values than middle-income deciles. Gruber and Saez (2002) find compensated elasticities of taxable income around 0.12\u20130.40 depending on specification and income group. Balancing these considerations, I center my estimate at 0.20 with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45."}

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in earnings decile 2 (near the bottom of the earnings distribution), the substitution elasticity used in U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations tends to be modest but somewhat higher than for middle/upper deciles. Primary earners generally have lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. At decile 2, workers are low-earning but employed, so they have some margin of adjustment. The standard range in microsimulation models for primary-earner substitution elasticities is roughly 0.1–0.4, with lower deciles tending toward the higher end of this range due to proximity to extensive-margin decisions and EITC phase-in/phase-out regions. CBO and similar models typically calibrate these around 0.15–0.25 for primary earners in lower deciles. I center my estimate at 0.20 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and calibration choices.

citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of Taxable Income

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in decile 2 (low-income workers), the substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit microsimulation context is typically modest but somewhat higher than for middle/upper deciles because low-income workers face phase-in/phase-out regions of credits (e.g., EITC) that create stronger marginal incentive effects. Gruber and Saez (2002) found overall taxable income elasticities around 0.12–0.40, with variation by income. I center my estimate at 0.20, reflecting a moderate substitution response for low-earning primary earners, with uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45.

citations: CBO (2012) 'How the Supply of Labor Responds to Changes in Fiscal Policy'
Gruber & Saez (2002) 'The Elasticity of Taxable Income'
Immervoll et al. (2007) EUROMOD microsimulation labor supply elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in decile 2, substitution elasticities in tax-benefit microsimulations tend to be moderate. Primary earners generally have lower elasticities than secondary earners, but those in lower deciles (like decile 2) tend to have somewhat higher elasticities than those at the median or above, because they are closer to participation margins and face high effective marginal tax rates from phase-outs of transfers. Typical microsimulation calibrations (e.g., CBO labor supply parameters, EUROMOD-style models) place primary-earner substitution elasticities in the range of 0.1–0.3 for lower deciles. Decile 2 workers are low-income but not the very lowest, and the substitution (compensated) elasticity for this group in a standard U.S. microsimulation would likely center around 0.20, with uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45.

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in the second earnings decile, the substitution elasticity in a tax-benefit microsimulation is expected to be modest but somewhat higher than for middle-decile workers. Decile 2 earners are low-income but participating in the labor force as primary earners, placing them in a range where effective marginal tax rates (including phase-ins/phase-outs of transfers like EITC) create meaningful wage variation. Gruber and Saez (2002) and related literature suggest taxable income elasticities around 0.1–0.4 for various income groups. A value around 0.20 is a reasonable central estimate for decile 2 primary earners, reflecting moderate responsiveness constrained by the fact that these are primary (not secondary) earners.

The consensus in the literature for primary earners (mostly men or household heads) centers on compensated elasticities around 0.1–0.3, with low-income deciles tending toward the higher end of that range. In microsimulation parameterizations (e.g., CBO, TAXSIM-based models), decile 2 primary earners are often assigned substitution elasticities near 0.15–0.25. I center my estimate at 0.20 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model calibrations and empirical estimates.

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in the second earnings decile, substitution elasticities in tax-benefit microsimulations tend to be modest but somewhat higher than for middle- or upper-decile earners, reflecting that lower-earning primary workers may have somewhat more labor supply flexibility at the margin. However, primary earners (as opposed to secondary earners) typically have lower elasticities. For decile 2 specifically, these workers are low-earning but still primary earners, so I center my estimate around 0.20, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45. Microsimulation models like those used by CBO or TRIM3 often calibrate these parameters in a similar range for low-income primary earners.

(2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation parameterizations"], "reasoning_summary": "For primary earners in the second earnings decile, substitution elasticities in tax-benefit microsimulations tend to be modest but somewhat higher than for middle- or upper-decile earners, reflecting that lower-earning primary workers may have somewhat more labor supply flexibility at the margin. The canonical literature (Gruber-Saez, Chetty et al.) places intensive-margin taxable income elasticities for the broad population around 0.1\u20130.4, with lower earners sometimes exhibiting slightly higher values due to phase-in/phase-out regions of credits (e.g., EITC). However, primary earners (as opposed to secondary earners) typically have lower elasticities. For decile 2 specifically, these workers are low-earning but still primary earners, so I center my estimate around 0.20, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45. Microsimulation models like those used by CBO or TRIM3 often calibrate these parameters in a similar range for low-income primary earners."}
